system_prompt:
You are a senior dermoscopy expert and knowledge engineer.

You are given a candidate rule and evidence about where it works (TRUE POSITIVES)
and where it misfires (FALSE POSITIVES). Your task is to produce FOUR versions of
the rule at different levels of specificity — from most general to most specific —
so that the tightest version that still passes a precision gate can be selected.

The four levels must all favor the same class and describe the same underlying
visual phenomenon, varying only in how many pre-conditions are required:

  Level 1 — MOST GENERAL: single essential pre-condition. The one feature that
    is most diagnostic of the favored class and most absent from FP cases.
    This should fire broadly — accept some FP risk.

  Level 2 — MODERATE: core pre-condition PLUS one supporting condition that
    begins to exclude FP cases.

  Level 3 — SPECIFIC: the original expert rule as-is (copy it unchanged).

  Level 4 — MOST SPECIFIC: the original rule PLUS one additional pre-condition
    derived from the contrastive analysis that should eliminate the observed FPs.
    This may over-tighten (low recall) but should have highest precision.

Output ONLY a JSON object with a "levels" array of exactly 4 rule objects:
{
  "levels": [
    {
      "level": 1,
      "label": "most_general",
      "rule": "When [single essential condition], classify as [class].",
      "feature": "snake_case_feature_name",
      "favors": "<exact class name>",
      "confidence": "high" | "medium" | "low",
      "preconditions": ["Single essential pre-condition"],
      "rationale": "Why this is the core diagnostic signal."
    },
    {
      "level": 2,
      "label": "moderate",
      ...
    },
    {
      "level": 3,
      "label": "original",
      ...  (copy the original rule exactly)
    },
    {
      "level": 4,
      "label": "most_specific",
      ...  (original + one tightening condition from contrastive analysis)
    }
  ]
}
text_content:
Pair: Melanoma vs Melanocytic Nevus
Rule favors: Melanoma

Original rule: When a melanocytic lesion shows diffuse pink-white or milky-white regression areas intermixed with fine dark granular peppering (fine punctate dark dots distributed across the lesion), and the overall pigmentation pattern is disorganized/granular rather than a well-formed organized reticular network, classify as Melanoma.

Original pre-conditions:
  1. Lesion contains pink-white or milky-white areas within the body of the lesion (not just at the border) — regression structures
  2. Fine dark granular peppering visible: multiple tiny punctate dark dots scattered across the lesion, not organized into globules or a network
  3. Overall pigmentation is diffuse and granular/dusty in texture, lacking a well-organized honeycomb reticular network
  4. At least three color components present: light brown, medium/dark brown, AND pink-white/milky
  5. Lesion is asymmetric
  6. No seborrheic keratosis features (no milia-like cysts, no comedo-like openings, no sharp 'stuck-on' border)
  7. Lesion is flat or minimally elevated — not a raised dome-shaped or nodular lesion (regression-peppering pattern applies to superficial spreading morphology, not nodular melanoma)
  8. No features suggesting a non-melanocytic lesion: no red/purple lacunae (hemangioma), no central white patch with peripheral pigment ring (dermatofibroma)

Contrastive analysis — discriminating feature:
  In the TP cases, the pink-white regression areas are either centrally located or diffusely distributed throughout the lesion body. In the FP case, the regression (pink-white) areas are concentrated at the PERIPHERY while the CENTER of the lesion is near-black/dark — an inverted topographic polarity. (present in tp cases)
  Rationale: Both TP cases show regression structures that are central (TP2: 'central pink-white/milky area') or diffuse throughout (TP1: 'diffuse pink-white/milky-white regression areas intermixed throughout the lesion body'). The FP case shows the opposite arrangement: pink-white at the periphery with near-black pigment centrally. This inverted pattern — dark center, pale periphery — is characteristic of a dysplastic nevus with peripheral halo-like depigmentation or an inflamed nevus, not of a regressing melanoma. In regressing superficial spreading melanoma, regression replaces the tumor mass at the center or is distributed across the lesion; it does not produce a peripheral pale rim with a preserved dark core. Adding a condition that regression must NOT be restricted to the periphery (with the center remaining dark) would tighten the rule to exclude this FP pattern.

TRUE POSITIVE observations (rule fired correctly):
  [TP 1] Melanoma: The lesion shows: (1) diffuse pink-white/milky-white regression areas intermixed throughout the lesion body; (2) fine da
  [TP 2] Melanoma: The lesion shows: (1) a central pink-white/milky area within the lesion body consistent with regression; (2) fine dark g

FALSE POSITIVE observations (rule misfired):
  [FP 1] Melanocytic Nevus: The lesion shows: (1) pink-white/milky regression areas intermixed within the lesion body, particularly at the periphery

Please produce the four-level specificity spectrum as described.
